question.text: Which of the following correctly describes the moratorium period in health insurance?

answer.text: After 3 continuous years of coverage, no claim can be contested by the insurer
After 5 continuous years of coverage, no claim can be contested including proven fraud
After 5 continuous years of coverage, no claim can be contested except on grounds of proven fraud and permanent exclusion
After 7 continuous years of coverage, claims become absolutely incontestable

explanation.text: **The Logic:** The health insurance moratorium period is 5 continuous years. After this period, no claim shall be contestable by the insurer except on grounds of proven fraud and permanent exclusion. This protects policyholders who have maintained continuous coverage. **The Trap:** Option A confuses the 3-year contestability period for life insurance (Section 45) with the health insurance moratorium. Option B is almost correct but removes the fraud/permanent exclusion exception, which always remains. **Cross-Exam Context:** The distinction between the 3-year incontestability for life insurance and the 5-year moratorium for health insurance is a high-frequency question in NISM Series X-B. Also note: if sum insured is enhanced, the 5-year moratorium starts afresh from the date of enhancement. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Advise clients to maintain continuous health insurance coverage without breaks. After 5 continuous years, they gain significant protection against claim denial. Switching insurers (portability) does not reset this clock if done properly.
